reasoning:
Let me think deeply about this problem through the lens of "paranucleic"—structures that orbit around a nucleus, enabling function without being central.

The core insight: traditional retirement assumes you save FROM income. But variable-income workers often work *more hours* than stable earners—they're not deficient in effort, just in predictable cash flow. What if we stopped trying to stabilize the unstable, and instead measured something else entirely?

```json
{
  "label": "Work-Hours Access Ledger",
  "design_principles": "Bypass savings entirely. Convert work-time to access rights, not currency. Income volatility becomes irrelevant when we measure hours, not dollars.",
  "core_mechanism": "Hours worked (any work) accumulate as access credits redeemable for housing, healthcare, food, transit in retirement—decoupling retirement from the unsolvable income-stabilization problem.",
  "how_it_works": "All work (gig, formal, caregiving) logged via app/employer verification. Hours convert to access credits at standard rates. Credits accumulate in public ledger. At retirement, credits unlock essential services from participating providers, reimbursed by collective fund. Implementation requires: work-logging infrastructure, provider network agreements, anti-fraud verification. Variable income irrelevant—only hours matter.",
  "what_is_new": "Existing systems try stabilizing unstable income for savings. This sidesteps savings by converting work-time directly to future access entitlements. The 'paranucleic' structure: retirement orbits your work-life rather than extracting from your volatile present.",
  "why_it_works": "Variable-income workers often work MORE hours than salaried workers. By measuring time-contribution rather than dollar-contribution, we capture actual effort. Access rights resist inflation and can't be lost to market crashes. Matches gig-economy reality.",
  "why_it_fails": "Massive verification burden—how do you prove informal work hours? Provider reimbursement rates become political. Wealthy could game the system by 'logging hours' while earning separately. May disincentivize efficiency (more hours = more credit).",
  "medium_term": "Essential services develop 'retirement access' tracks. Work-logging normalizes. Gig platforms compete on 'access multipliers.' Informal economy slowly formalizes to capture credits.",
  "long_term_vision": "Retirement universalized for anyone who worked sufficient hours, regardless of income stability. The 'savings problem' dissolves—money earned is for living now; access rights are for living later. Retirement becomes about time given, not wealth accumulated."
}
```

solution.label: Work-Hours Access Ledger
solution.design_principles: Bypass savings entirely. Convert work-time to access rights, not currency. Income volatility becomes irrelevant when we measure hours, not dollars.
solution.core_mechanism: Hours worked (any work) accumulate as access credits redeemable for housing, healthcare, food, transit in retirement—decoupling retirement from the unsolvable income-stabilization problem.
solution.how_it_works: All work (gig, formal, caregiving) logged via app/employer verification. Hours convert to access credits at standard rates. Credits accumulate in public ledger. At retirement, credits unlock essential services from participating providers, reimbursed by collective fund. Implementation requires: work-logging infrastructure, provider network agreements, anti-fraud verification. Variable income irrelevant—only hours matter.
solution.what_is_new: Existing systems try stabilizing unstable income for savings. This sidesteps savings by converting work-time directly to future access entitlements. The 'paranucleic' structure: retirement orbits your work-life rather than extracting from your volatile present.
solution.why_it_works: Variable-income workers often work MORE hours than salaried workers. By measuring time-contribution rather than dollar-contribution, we capture actual effort. Access rights resist inflation and can't be lost to market crashes. Matches gig-economy reality.
solution.why_it_fails: Massive verification burden—how do you prove informal work hours? Provider reimbursement rates become political. Wealthy could game the system by 'logging hours' while earning separately. May disincentivize efficiency (more hours = more credit).
solution.medium_term: Essential services develop 'retirement access' tracks. Work-logging normalizes. Gig platforms compete on 'access multipliers.' Informal economy slowly formalizes to capture credits.
solution.long_term_vision: Retirement universalized for anyone who worked sufficient hours, regardless of income stability. The 'savings problem' dissolves—money earned is for living now; access rights are for living later. Retirement becomes about time given, not wealth accumulated.
